pathways.label: Automatic expungement under D.C. Code § 16-802
pathways.summary: DC automatic expungement covers criminal records and court proceedings related only to citations, arrests, charges, or convictions for an offense that was later decriminalized, legalized, or held unconstitutional by the DC Court of Appeals or the U.S. Supreme Court. It also covers records related only to simple possession of any quantity of marijuana under 48-904.01(d)(1) before February 15, 2015, if the case reached final disposition and the prosecutor has not successfully moved to retain or contest the record. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-802") The statute says eligible records are to be expunged by October…
ruleClauses: DC automatic expungement covers criminal records and court proceedings related only to citations, arrests, charges, or convictions for an offense that was later decriminalized, legalized, or held unconstitutional by the DC Court of Appeals or the U.S. Supreme Court. It also covers records related only to simple possession of any quantity of marijuana under 48-904.01(d)(1) before February 15, 2015, if the case reached final disposition and the prosecutor has not successfully moved to retain or contest the record. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-802")

The statute says eligible records are to be expunged by October 1, 2027, or within 90 days after termination/final disposition, whichever is later. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-802")

DC has an automatic expungement law for certain decriminalized, legalized, unconstitutional, and old simple marijuana possession records. But DC Courts currently says automatic sealing and expungement are not yet operating, so we should verify the record and be prepared to file a motion if needed.

Automatic sealing D.C. Code 16-805 Non-convictions and eligible misdemeanor convictions after 10 years
Sealing by motion D.C. Code 16-806 Non-convictions, eligible convictions, fugitive-from-justice records, mistaken/incorrect identity records
Juvenile sealing D.C. Code 16-2335 Juvenile delinquency / Family Court records

Wilma should therefore treat automatic relief as a statutory pathway to verify, not a process to promise. (

Automatic sealing D.C. Code 16-805 Non-convictions and eligible misdemeanor convictions after 10 years
exclusionRules: Conviction sealing by motion For conviction records, 16-806 allows sealing by motion if: For a misdemeanor, at least 5 years have passed since completion of sentence. For a felony, at least 8 years have passed since completion of sentence. Offense Severity Group 1, 2, or 3 felonies on the DC Sentencing Commission Master Grid are not eligible. The person proves by a preponderance of the evidence that sealing is in the interests of justice. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-806")

Effect of sealing After sealing, the record is removed from public view, and the person cannot be held guilty of perjury or false statement for failing to disclose the citation, arrest, charge, prosecution, disposition, or conviction in response to an inquiry. Public agencies must reply to public inquiries that no records are available. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-807") But sealed records remain available in nonpublic form to the person or counsel; prosecutors, defense attorneys, law enforcement, corrections, pretrial, and supervision agencies for lawful purposes; certain licensing/employment entities where allowed; civil litigation related to the matter; and by court order for good cause. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-807")

Effect of expungement Expungement restores the person to the status occupied before the citation, arrest, charge, or conviction, and the person cannot be treated as committing perjury or making a false statement for failing to disclose the record. Agencies must remove public references and respond to public inquiries that no records are available. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-804") However, the statute allows the clerk, prosecutors, law enforcement, corrections, pretrial, and community supervision agencies to retain restricted nonpublic records, and those records may be accessed by the person/counsel, criminal justice actors for limited lawful purposes, civil litigation connected to the matter, or by court order for good cause. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-804")

Automatic sealing - 16-805 DC automatic sealing is written to cover: 1 Citations, arrests, or charges where the case ended without conviction or acquittal; and 2 Misdemeanor convictions where at least 10 years have passed since completion of sentence. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-805") But automatic sealing is not available for listed categories, including intrafamily offenses, parental kidnapping, criminal abuse or financial exploitation of vulnerable adults/elderly persons, certain child neglect offenses, incest, misdemeanor sexual abuse, certain sex/obscenity/prostitution-related chapters, unexpired sex-offender-registration offenses, dangerous crimes, crimes of violence, DUI, commercial-vehicle DUI, and operating while impaired. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-805") The statute says automatically sealed records should be sealed by October 1, 2027, or within 90 days after the triggering event, whichever is later. Offense Severity Group 1, 2, or 3 felonies on the DC Sentencing Commission Master Grid are not eligible.

DC now separates expungement and sealing.
Expungement is the stronger remedy. Statutorily, expungement restores the person,

to the status occupied before the citation, arrest, charge, or conviction. But Wilma should not overpromise total destruction because the statute still allows certain nonpublic restricted records to be retained and accessed for limited purposes. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-804")

Sealing removes records from public view and allows restricted nonpublic access by specific parties for specific purposes. Agencies must eliminate public references, but they may retain nonpublic restricted-access records. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-807")

In DC, expungement is stronger than sealing, but neither should be described as making the record disappear from every government system forever. Sealing hides the record from public view. sourceSections.title: Core DC rule
sourceSections.text:
DC now separates expungement and sealing.
Expungement is the stronger remedy. Statutorily, expungement restores the person,
in the contemplation of the law,
to the status occupied before the citation, arrest, charge, or conviction. But Wilma should not overpromise total destruction because the statute still allows certain nonpublic restricted records to be retained and accessed for limited purposes. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-804")
Sealing removes records from public view and allows restricted nonpublic access by specific parties for specific purposes. Agencies must eliminate public references, but they may retain nonpublic restricted-access records. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-807")
Wilma plain-language script:
In DC, expungement is stronger than sealing, but neither should be described as making the record disappear from every government system forever. Sealing hides the record from public view. Expungement can restore your legal status as if the event did not occur, but some nonpublic access may still exist under DC law.

sourceSections.title: Main DC pathways
sourceSections.text:
Pathway Authority Best used for
Automatic expungement D.C. Code 16-802 Decriminalized/legalized/unconstitutional offenses and old simple marijuana possession
Expungement by motion D.C. Code 16-803 Actual innocence
Automatic sealing D.C. Code 16-805 Non-convictions and eligible misdemeanor convictions after 10 years
Sealing by motion D.C. Code 16-806 Non-convictions, eligible convictions, fugitive-from-justice records, mistaken/incorrect identity records
Juvenile sealing D.C. Code 16-2335 Juvenile delinquency / Family Court records
Important operational warning: DCs statutory automatic sealing/expungement provisions exist in the Code, but the DC Courts page currently says the automatic provisions in the Second Chance Amendment Act of 2022 are not yet in effect and that no cases are currently being automatically sealed or expunged. Wilma should therefore treat automatic relief as a statutory pathway to verify, not a process to promise. (

"https://www.dccourts.gov/superior-court/superior-court-divisions/criminal-division/sealing-criminal-records")

sourceSections.title: Automatic expungement - 16-802
sourceSections.text:
DC automatic expungement covers criminal records and court proceedings related only to citations, arrests, charges, or convictions for an offense that was later decriminalized, legalized, or held unconstitutional by the DC Court of Appeals or the U.S. Supreme Court. It also covers records related only to simple possession of any quantity of marijuana under 48-904.01(d)(1) before February 15, 2015, if the case reached final disposition and the prosecutor has not successfully moved to retain or contest the record. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-802")
The statute says eligible records are to be expunged by October 1, 2027, or within 90 days after termination/final disposition, whichever is later. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-802")
Wilma status labels:
possible_auto_expungement_decriminalized_offense
possible_auto_expungement_legalized_offense
possible_auto_expungement_unconstitutional_offense
possible_auto_expungement_marijuana_simple_possession_pre_2015_02_15
auto_process_not_currently_operational_verify_dc_courts
prosecutor_retention_or_contest_possible
Wilma script:
DC has an automatic expungement law for certain decriminalized, legalized, unconstitutional, and old simple marijuana possession records. But DC Courts currently says automatic sealing and expungement are not yet operating, so we should verify the record and be prepared to file a motion if needed.

sourceSections.title: Expungement by motion - actual innocence, 16-803
sourceSections.text:
A person may file a written motion for expungement based on actual innocence if the case ended without conviction or acquittal, and the person proves by a preponderance of the evidence that the offense either did not occur or was not committed by the person. The motion may be filed at any time and must be served on the prosecutor. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-803")
The court may deny the motion on initial review, decide whether a hearing is needed, order a prosecutor response, or hold a hearing. If granted, the court must summarize the factual circumstances and rule as a matter of law that the person did not commit the offense or that no offense occurred. Eligible records must be expunged within 90 days after the motion is granted. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-803")
An acquittal or dismissal does not automatically create a presumption of innocence for this motion. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-803")
Wilma status labels:
possible_actual_innocence_expungement
actual_innocence_offense_did_not_occur
actual_innocence_wrong_person
needs_actual_innocence_evidence
needs_prosecutor_service
not_automatic_acquittal_or_dismissal_no_presumption
Evidence Wilma should ask for:
Dismissal order or final disposition.
Police report.
Witness affidavit.
Victim recantation or affidavit, if applicable.
Alibi evidence.
Identity-mistake evidence.
Prosecutor correspondence.
Any court finding showing the person did not commit the offense.

sourceSections.title: Automatic sealing - 16-805
sourceSections.text:
DC automatic sealing is written to cover:
1 Citations, arrests, or charges where the case ended without conviction or acquittal; and
2 Misdemeanor convictions where at least 10 years have passed since completion of sentence. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-805")
But automatic sealing is not available for listed categories, including intrafamily offenses, parental kidnapping, criminal abuse or financial exploitation of vulnerable adults/elderly persons, certain child neglect offenses, incest, misdemeanor sexual abuse, certain sex/obscenity/prostitution-related chapters, unexpired sex-offender-registration offenses, dangerous crimes, crimes of violence, DUI, commercial-vehicle DUI, and operating while impaired. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-805")
The statute says automatically sealed records should be sealed by October 1, 2027, or within 90 days after the triggering event, whichever is later. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-805")
Wilma status labels:
possible_auto_sealing_non_conviction
possible_auto_sealing_misdemeanor_10_years
auto_sealing_excluded_offense
auto_process_not_currently_operational_verify_dc_courts
Wilma script:
DC has an automatic sealing framework for many non-convictions and some misdemeanor convictions after 10 years, but the court currently says automatic sealing is not yet operating. For now, we should verify the record and consider a motion-based sealing route.

sourceSections.title: Non-conviction sealing by motion
sourceSections.text:
The court shall order sealing of citations, arrests, and charges when the case ended without conviction or acquittal, if the motion demonstrates by a preponderance of the evidence that sealing is in the interests of justice. Some temporary-version rules apply through September 11, 2026, so Wilma should verify the current text before filing later in 2026. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-806")
Wilma status labels:
possible_motion_sealing_non_conviction
needs_interests_of_justice_showing
temporary_law_verify_current_text_before_filing

sourceSections.title: Fugitive-from-justice sealing
sourceSections.text:
A DC fugitive-from-justice arrest may be sealed if the person was arrested in DC as a fugitive, waived extradition hearing and was released or detained under the statute, the fugitive case reached final disposition, the person appeared before the proper official in the other jurisdiction, and sealing is in the interests of justice. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-806")
Wilma status label:possible_fugitive_from_justice_sealing

sourceSections.title: Conviction sealing by motion
sourceSections.text:
For conviction records, 16-806 allows sealing by motion if:
For a misdemeanor, at least 5 years have passed since completion of sentence.
For a felony, at least 8 years have passed since completion of sentence.
Offense Severity Group 1, 2, or 3 felonies on the DC Sentencing Commission Master Grid are not eligible.
The person proves by a preponderance of the evidence that sealing is in the interests of justice. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-806")

Completion of the sentence
means unconditional discharge from incarceration, commitment, probation, parole, or supervised release, whichever is latest. Nonpayment of fines, restitution, or other monetary assessments does not prevent sentence completion under the definition. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-801")
The prosecutor may waive the conviction waiting periods in writing. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-806")
Wilma status labels:

possible_motion_sealing_felony_8_years
not_eligible_osg_1_2_3_felony
not_eligible_yet_waiting_period

sourceSections.text:
A sealing motion must state all of the persons unsealed and unexpunged citations, arrests, charges, and convictions as reasonably known; the grounds for eligibility; and facts supporting the claim. If the motion is incomplete, the court gives the person 30 days to amend before dismissing without prejudice. A copy must be served on the prosecutor. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-806")
The court may deny the motion on initial review, decide whether a hearing is required, order a prosecutor response, and set deadlines. At a hearing, both the movant and prosecutor may present witnesses and information, and hearsay is admissible. An order must be written, include reasons, be final for appeal purposes, and generally be issued within 180 days, unless good cause exists for delay. If granted, sealing must occur within 90 days after the motion is granted. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-806")

sourceSections.title: Effect of sealing
sourceSections.text:
After sealing, the record is removed from public view, and the person cannot be held guilty of perjury or false statement for failing to disclose the citation, arrest, charge, prosecution, disposition, or conviction in response to an inquiry. Public agencies must reply to public inquiries that no records are available. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-807")
But sealed records remain available in nonpublic form to the person or counsel; prosecutors, defense attorneys, law enforcement, corrections, pretrial, and supervision agencies for lawful purposes; certain licensing/employment entities where allowed; civil litigation related to the matter; and by court order for good cause. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-807")
Wilma script:
Sealing can let you answer most public-facing questions as if the record does not exist, but the record can still be accessed in specific legal, criminal justice, licensing, employment, civil litigation, or court-approved situations.

sourceSections.title: Effect of expungement
sourceSections.text:
Expungement restores the person to the status occupied before the citation, arrest, charge, or conviction, and the person cannot be treated as committing perjury or making a false statement for failing to disclose the record. Agencies must remove public references and respond to public inquiries that no records are available. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-804")
However, the statute allows the clerk, prosecutors, law enforcement, corrections, pretrial, and community supervision agencies to retain restricted nonpublic records, and those records may be accessed by the person/counsel, criminal justice actors for limited lawful purposes, civil litigation connected to the matter, or by court order for good cause. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-804")
Wilma caution:
Even for expungement, do not promise that no government actor can ever access the record again.

sourceSections.title: Juvenile record sealing - 16-2335
sourceSections.text:
DC juvenile records may be sealed on motion by the person, or on the Family Divisions own motion, if:
The person has reached majority if the case involved neglect; or
Two years have passed since final discharge from custody/supervision, or since entry of another Division order not involving custody/supervision; and
The person has not later been convicted of a crime or adjudicated delinquent or in need of supervision; and
No proceeding is pending seeking such conviction or adjudication. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-2335")
When juvenile sealing is granted, the proceedings are treated as if they never occurred, and all facts relating to arrest, petition, adjudication, filing, and disposition no longer exist as a matter of law. The person may reply that no record exists. But a later delinquency, need-of-supervision adjudication, or felony conviction nullifies the sealing order. ("https://code.dccouncil.gov/us/dc/council/code/sections/16-2335")
Wilma status labels:

sourceSections.title: Packet logic for RCAP/Wilma
sourceSections.text:
Actual innocence expungement packet
Generate/check:
1 Written motion for expungement under D.C. Code 16-803.
2 Statement of actual innocence grounds.
3 Facts showing the offense did not occur or was not committed by the person.
4 Exhibits, affidavits, police reports, dismissal records, witness statements, or identity evidence.
5 Case disposition.
6 MPD arrest history report.
7 Certificate/service on prosecutor.
8 Proposed order.
Sealing by motion packet
Generate/check:
1 Written motion to seal under D.C. Code 16-806.
2 Complete list of all reasonably known unsealed/unexpunged citations, arrests, charges, and convictions.
3 Eligibility basis: non-conviction, misdemeanor 5-year, felony 8-year, fugitive record, or identity correction.
4 Completion-of-sentence proof, if conviction.
5 Interests-of-justice statement.
6 Rehabilitation and reintegration evidence.
7 Victim/prosecutor-position issue, if any.
8 MPD arrest history report.
9 DC Superior Court disposition.
10 Certificate/service on prosecutor.
11 Proposed order.
Automatic pathway tracking packet
Because DC Courts currently says automatic relief is not yet operating, Wilma should create a tracking file, not a promise:
1 Identify possible automatic expungement or sealing category.
2 Verify case in MPD arrest history and DC Superior Court disposition.

1 Motion to seal juvenile records under D.C. Code 16-2335.

sourceSections.title: Attorney/legal-aid referral triggers
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should recommend legal review if:
The user wants actual innocence expungement.
Any felony conviction is involved.
Felony Offense Severity Group needs to be determined.
The record involves sex, violence, intrafamily offense, vulnerable adult/elder abuse, child offense, DUI, gun registration, or dangerous crime.
The case has co-defendants and redaction is needed.
The record is federal, military, tribal, or outside DC.
The person has immigration, firearm, licensing, security-clearance, housing, childcare, healthcare, or law-enforcement employment concerns.
Automatic relief appears available but has not happened.
The prosecutor contests or seeks to retain records.
Juvenile sealing could be nullified by later adjudication or felony conviction.

17. Wilma
never say
rules
Wilma must not say:

Your DC record will definitely be erased.

Nobody can ever see it again.

Automatic sealing is already taking care of it.

A dismissal proves actual innocence.

You never have to disclose this anywhere.

This fixes immigration consequences.

This restores firearm rights.

This clears federal records.

Every misdemeanor is automatically sealed after 10 years.

Every felony can be sealed after 8 years.
